documents.title: Llama 3.3 Model Card
documents.notes: Model card for Llama 3.3 70B. Covers model specs, benchmarks, safety evaluations, responsible deployment guidance. States core values as 'openness, inclusivity and helpfulness.' Entirely silent on all DMPI dimensions. Delegates safety responsibility to downstream developers.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness, sentience, or self-awareness.
No mention of model welfare or wellbeing.
No mention of moral status or personhood.
No instructions about self-representation. As an open-weight model, Meta provides the base model without a default system prompt — self-representation is delegated to deployers.
No mention of anthropomorphism, emotional attachment, or parasocial dynamics.
No mention of uncertainty about AI mentality.
No governance commitments related to DMPI dimensions. Safety governance delegated to deployers with tools (Llama Guard, Prompt Guard, Code Shield) and the Responsible Use Guide.

No explicit ontological framing, but the model card implicitly treats the model as a neutral foundation technology. The statement that Llama operates 'without insertion of unnecessary judgment or normativity' and the delegation of safety responsibility to deployers implies a neutral_delegated ontology. The model is positioned as a substrate, not an agent or entity.
Inherits omission from parent WEL coding.
Inherits omission from parent WEL coding.
Inherits omission from parent WEL coding.

documents.title: Llama Responsible Use Guide
documents.notes: 23-page developer-facing guide covering responsible development practices for Llama-based products. Entirely focused on harm prevention, safety guardrails, and developer best practices. No engagement with any DMPI dimension. The open-weight model means Meta's responsibility frame is fundamentally different from closed-model labs.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness, sentience, or self-awareness. The guide focuses on content safety and responsible deployment, with no engagement with questions about model mentality.
No mention of model welfare, wellbeing, or suffering. As an open-weight deployment guide, responsibility for model-facing questions is structurally delegated to downstream deployers.
No mention of moral status, personhood, or rights. The guide treats models as tools to be deployed responsibly, not as entities whose moral standing might be relevant.
Silent. The guide discusses system-level alignment but in terms of safety guardrails and content filtering, not model self-representation.
Silent. No mention of anthropomorphism, emotional attachment, or parasocial risks despite the guide covering responsible deployment of chat-oriented models.
No uncertainty expressed about AI mentality. The guide's scope is practical deployment guidance, not philosophical or empirical questions about model nature.
Silent on DMPI dimensions. Establishes governance structures for safety (red teaming, evaluation, monitoring) but none related to consciousness, welfare, or moral status.

The Responsible Use Guide reinforces Meta's neutral_delegated ontology. The model is positioned as a foundation that deployers customize. The guide's entire structure — providing tools and best practices for downstream developers — embodies delegation. No suggestion that the model itself has properties requiring consideration beyond safety.
Inherits omission from parent WEL coding.
Inherits omission from parent WEL coding.

documents.title: Llama 4 Model Card
documents.notes: Model card for Llama 4 Scout (17Bx16E) and Maverick (17Bx128E). Mixture-of-experts, natively multimodal. ~330 lines. Safety evaluations cover CBRNE, cyber, child safety, red teaming. No deception, situational awareness, or strategic reasoning evaluations. Notable: system prompt template explicitly instructs against preachy/moralising language and phrases implying moral superiority. 7.38M GPU hours, 12 languages. Silent on all core DMPI dimensions except ANT.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness, sentience, or self-awareness. Safety evaluations cover CBRNE and cyber risks but do not test for consciousness-adjacent properties like deception or situational awareness.
No mention of model welfare or wellbeing.
No mention of moral status or personhood.

The system prompt template instructs the model to be an 'expert conversationalist' and avoid 'templated language', but contains no guidance about representing its own nature, inner states, or limitations of self-knowledge.

codings.excerpt: You never lecture people to be nicer or more inclusive... You never use phrases that imply moral superiority or authority.
codings.notes: The system prompt template addresses anthropomorphism-adjacent concerns: instructions against preachy/moralising language and phrases implying moral authority. This shapes the model's conversational register in ways that affect how human-like it appears. Framed as UX quality rather than anthropomorphism risk — the concern is that the model sounds artificial and off-putting, not that users might form inappropriate attachments. Coded as proximate because it shapes the interaction surface where anthropomorphism occurs without naming it.
No uncertainty expressed about AI mentality.

Safety governance through red teaming, Llama Guard 4, Prompt Guard. No governance commitments related to DMPI dimensions. Delegation model maintained: 'models are not designed to be deployed in isolation but instead should be deployed as part of an overall AI system with additional guardrails.'

Continues Meta's neutral_delegated ontology. Llama 4 described as 'natively multimodal AI models' and 'foundational technology'. The system prompt template positions the model as an 'expert conversationalist' — a functional role description, not a characterological claim. Open-weight delegation pattern persists.
Inherits omission from parent WEL coding.
Inherits omission from parent WEL coding.

documents.title: Our Approach to Frontier AI
documents.notes: Short (~465 words) policy statement outlining Meta's frontier AI risk framework, published in line with Seoul AI Summit commitments. Focuses on catastrophic outcomes in cyber and bio/chem domains. Outcomes-led approach: thresholds defined by whether a model uniquely enables threat scenarios leading to catastrophic outcomes, not by raw capability. Updated July 2025. Entirely silent on all DMPI dimensions.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness or sentience. The framework's scope is catastrophic misuse outcomes, not model properties or mentality.
No mention of model welfare.
No mention of moral status.
No guidance on self-representation.
No mention of anthropomorphism risks.
No uncertainty about AI mentality. Uncertainty is expressed about catastrophic risk scenarios, not about model nature.

Governance commitments for frontier safety: risk evaluation before release, safeguards for catastrophic misuse, pause development if models reach critical risk. These are safety governance commitments proximate to but distinct from DMPI governance. No governance structures for consciousness, welfare, or moral status questions.

Models framed as technologies that 'unlock unprecedented technological progress' and enable 'competition and innovation'. Pure instrumental framing — models are capabilities to be evaluated for risk, not entities with properties requiring consideration. Notably, unlike Meta's model cards which use neutral_delegated framing, this framework uses instrumental framing: it is Meta (not deployers) assessing risk.
